segments.title: Bruships 246 waits for the questions-open sign
segments.summary: The stream setup tells viewers not to post questions until the questions-open sign is visible.
segments.body: The stream sets up the live question system before the naval-history answers begin. The passage is useful context for the live Q&A format, but the substantive naval material starts after the questions-open sign appears.

evidence.note: Transcript advises waiting for the questions-open sign and then continues stream setup.

segments.title: Royal Navy destroyer count question
segments.summary: A Q&A segment works through destroyer counts, including World War I and possible World War II destroyers.
segments.body: The answer corrects and refines the count while discussing Royal Navy destroyer totals.

evidence.note: A Q&A segment works through destroyer counts, including World War I and possible World War II destroyers.

segments.question: How many destroyers are being counted in this Royal Navy scenario?
segments.answerShort: The answer works through the count and adjusts it upward as omitted destroyers are considered.

segments.title: Japanese naval history Jeopardy question
segments.summary: A viewer asks how a Japanese naval-history Jeopardy question set should rate questions.
segments.body: The answer considers question difficulty and references naval actions as possible material.

evidence.note: A viewer asks how a Japanese naval-history Jeopardy question set should rate questions.

segments.question: If making a naval-history Japanese question set, how would you rate the individual questions?
segments.answerShort: The answer would vary by difficulty and topic, with Japanese naval actions providing possible question material.

segments.title: Clemenceau versus Midway question
segments.summary: A question asks whether Clemenceau with improved aircraft would have been as good as Midway.
segments.body: The answer says not as good as Midway, but potentially much better than the baseline comparison.

evidence.note: A question asks whether Clemenceau with improved aircraft would have been as good as Midway.

segments.question: Would Clemenceau with improved aircraft have been as good as the Midways?
segments.answerShort: Not as good as Midway, but the answer allows that the improved version could be significantly better than otherwise.

segments.title: British submarine aircraft carrier question
segments.summary: A viewer proposes a British submarine aircraft carrier with multiple aircraft.
segments.body: The host likes the question and flags it as worth preserving, making it a clear alternate-history Q&A entry.

evidence.note: A viewer proposes a British submarine aircraft carrier with multiple aircraft.

segments.question: If you were a naval architect in Britain, what about a submarine aircraft carrier with four aircraft?
segments.answerShort: The question is treated as an interesting alternate design prompt worth keeping.

segments.title: Important point about naval power
segments.summary: A question asks for an important point about naval power, leading into naval strike missiles and ship roles.
segments.body: The answer connects naval power to practical weapon and platform constraints.

evidence.note: A question asks for an important point about naval power, leading into naval strike missiles and ship roles.

segments.question: What is an important point about naval power?
segments.answerShort: The answer links naval power to practical platform, missile, and force-employment limits rather than slogans.

segments.title: Trafalgar and having another fleet question
segments.summary: A question around Trafalgar leads to a point that losing Nelson would still leave Britain with another fleet.
segments.body: The answer emphasizes the strategic importance of fleet depth and reserves.

evidence.note: A question around Trafalgar leads to a point that losing Nelson would still leave Britain with another fleet.

segments.question: What changes if Nelson loses at Trafalgar?
segments.answerShort: The answer notes Britain still has another fleet, so the strategic context is larger than one commander or one battle.
